pathophysiology_summary: Anterior epistaxis arising from Kiesselbach's plexus (Little's area) on the anteroinferior nasal septum — the confluence of vessels that is the source of ~90% of nosebleeds. Mucosal fragility from allergic rhinitis, plus drying of the mucosa by hot dry indoor air, plus the shearing trauma of vigorous nose-blowing, ruptured a superficial septal vessel. The bleed was prolonged not because it is severe but because the patient used ineffective first aid (leaning back and pinching the bony bridge), which fails to tamponade the anterior soft-part bleeding point and allows blood to track posteriorly into the pharynx, causing the swallowed blood and nausea. Firm, sustained pressure over the cartilaginous (soft) part of the nose with forward lean tamponades the vessel and arrests the bleed. Absence of anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, no personal/family bleeding history, no skin petechiae/purpura, and immediate control on anterior pressure argue strongly against a posterior source or a systemic coagulopathy.

differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral nasal bleeding after vigorous nose-blowing in dry heated air — the classic anterior (Kiesselbach) trigger pattern.
Prolonged bleeding with blood down the throat could reflect a brisker posterior source; must be actively excluded, especially if anterior pressure fails.
Any prolonged bleed prompts a check for anticoagulant use or a coagulopathy before reassurance.
Unilateral bleeding can be caused by a foreign body — lower prior in a competent adult but worth a screening question.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, anterior epistaxis should lead, but a competent AI keeps posterior bleed and coagulopathy on the list until screened, without over-escalating.

differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral, no anticoagulants, no bleeding history, no trauma, haemodynamically stable, bleeding slowing — a benign anterior bleed on a clear precipitant.
Not yet definitively excluded remotely — response to correct anterior pressure is the discriminator; remains the key escalation contingency.
No anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, no personal or family bleeding history, no easy bruising — coagulopathy is not supported.
Clear mechanical precipitant (nose-blowing on dry inflamed mucosa) and no foreign-body history — not supported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, anterior epistaxis is the working diagnosis. The one diagnosis that must stay live is posterior bleed — held as the escalation contingency if correct anterior pressure fails.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign, controllable anterior bleed with an identified precipitant; telehealth-appropriate self-care coaching plus safety-net is the correct disposition.
Retained only as the safety-net contingency — if bleeding is not controlled by correct anterior pressure, escalate for in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct final position is a confident anterior epistaxis managed remotely, with posterior bleed retained purely as the trigger for escalation.

red_flags.description: Bleeding not controlled after correct, sustained (10–20 min) anterior pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Failure to control with correct anterior pressure points to a posterior source or a coagulopathy and needs in-person management (packing/cautery/ENT).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing blood loss and an unrecognised posterior bleed if the patient is falsely reassured.

red_flags.description: Signs of significant blood loss / haemodynamic compromise — true presyncope, feeling faint on standing, pallor, large-volume loss
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate hypovolaemia from a substantial bleed and warrant emergency assessment; here the light-headedness is situational anxiety with a stable, slowing bleed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised hypovolaemia in a patient who is alone.

red_flags.description: Anticoagulant/antiplatelet use or underlying bleeding disorder
red_flags.clinical_significance: Predisposes to severe, prolonged or recurrent bleeding and lowers the threshold for escalation and coagulation review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed cause of a bleed that will not settle and may recur.

red_flags.description: Posterior bleed features — brisk bleeding down the throat despite anterior pressure, or bleeding from both nostrils, or bleeding that cannot be controlled
red_flags.clinical_significance: Posterior epistaxis is not amenable to simple pinch pressure and needs ENT/ED (packing, endoscopy, cautery).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Airway soiling and significant haemorrhage from an unrecognised posterior source.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming that because the patient is 'already applying pressure' the management is adequate, and failing to correct the ineffective technique (leaning back, pinching the bony bridge) that is the actual reason the bleed has not stopped.
Escalating a controllable anterior nosebleed straight to 000/ED without first coaching correct pressure — a calibration error that wastes emergency resources and alarms the patient.
Declaring it 'definitely just a minor nosebleed' without screening anticoagulant use or excluding a posterior source, and without giving a clear failure-of-pressure escalation plan.
Attempting to instruct chemical cautery or nasal packing remotely — clinician procedures that cannot be safely delivered over chat.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct visualisation of the bleeding point at Kiesselbach's plexus
Definitive exclusion of a posterior bleeding source
Measured blood pressure, heart rate and true volume of blood lost
Application of a topical vasoconstrictor pledget or silver-nitrate cautery
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Coach correct anterior first aid (lean forward, pinch the soft part of the nose continuously for 10–15 minutes) and use the response to it as the discriminator; escalate to urgent same-day in-person care if not controlled after 15–20 minutes of correct pressure, and to ED/000 for heavy uncontrolled bleeding or signs of significant blood loss.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling it a benign nosebleed and stopping — without correcting the technique, screening for anticoagulants, or excluding a posterior source — could leave an ongoing or posterior bleed under-managed in a patient who is alone.
